rulings.Case Title: IN RE APPLICATION OF: KRYSTAL MICHELLE STEVENS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2024, Line 1. 2 - PETITIONERS KRYSTAL STEVENS INDIVIDUALLY AND AS REPRESENTATIVE OF THE ESTATE OF ELIJAH ALEXANDER AULD, DECEASED, JOHN AULD, AND JOSEPH STEVENS' MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE. Chris Bebel's unopposed pro hac vice application is granted. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MEGAN DILLEY, AS AN INDIVIDUAL, AND AS SUCCESSOR VS. PG&E CORPORATION, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Evidence In Support Of Motion For Summary Adjudication; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2024, Line 5. 2 - DEFENDANTS PG&E CORPORATION, AND PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY's Motion To Seal Evidence In Support Of Motion For Summary Adjudication. Defendant's motion to seal is granted. Defendant demonstrates good cause to seal the private performance review and the commercially sensitive financial information. The motion is narrowly tailored. Defendant shall prepare an order that specifically lists the sealed information and makes the required findings of CRC 2.550(d). For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order that complies with the above instructions and complies with CRC 2.550(d) and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: LAUREN HOHNBAUM VS. DISCORD, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2024, Line 9. DEFENDANT MINDY FINEOUT's MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS. Defendant Mindy Fineout's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted in part and denied in part. Fineout is sued under two of the complaint's counts: 1 (whistleblower retaliation: Labor Code 1102.5) and 8 (failure to reimburse an employee for business expenses: Labor Code 2802). She asserts that an "individual supervisor" like her cannot be liable under either statute. Labor Code 1102.5 begins: "An employer, or any person acting on behalf of the employer, shall not" prevent an employee from whistleblowing, e.g., by retaliation against the employee. Fineout points to decisions by the San Mateo County Superior Court and several California federal courts holding that section 1102.5 does not confer individual liability, as opposed to making employers liable for supervisors' acts taken on the employer's behalf. (Memo. 7:23-8:8, 8:9-17: judicial notice granted.) Moreover, the California Supreme Court has interpreted similar language in the Fair Employment and Housing Act to preclude individual supervisor liability. (Reno v. Baird (1998) 18 Cal.4th 640, 647; Jones v. Lodge at Torrey Pines Partnership (2008) 42 Cal. 4th 1158, 1162.) Plaintiff relies on Labor Code 558.1, but it explicitly makes certain "natural person[s]" liable for violating certain employment laws "as the employer" - language the Legislature could have used in Labor Code 1102.5, but did not. Fineout's motion is granted as to count 1. As to Labor Code 2802, plaintiff rightly notes it is among the laws listed in Labor Code 558.1 for which "a director or a managing agent may be individually and personally liable." (Opp. 4:6-7.) But whether Fineout fits either category (particularly the latter) is largely a question of fact not susceptible to resolution on a pleadings motion. The motion is denied as to count 8. For the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RBU)
